channel.title: Psychology Today: The Latest
channel.description: The latest blog posts from Psychology Today

item.title: The Importance of a Few Good Friends
item.description: Personal Perspective: I’m grateful for the friends I’ve had and still have, and for the books and songs that have befriended me.

media:content.media:description: The Importance of a Few Good Friends

item.dc:creator: Madora M Kibbe

item.title: Multi-Determinism in Eating Disorders
item.description: Theoretical approaches, treatment preferences, and clinician bias all play a role in the ways we understand eating disorders.

media:content.media:description: Multi-Determinism in Eating Disorders

item.dc:creator: Judy Scheel Ph.D., L.C.S.W., CEDS, PLLC

item.title: Can a Boom in Manufacturing Lead to Mental Health Problems?
item.description: Workers with less education or experience tend not to fare as well during economic boom-bust cycles, research suggests—and their mental health may suffer as a result.

media:content.media:description: Can a Boom in Manufacturing Lead to Mental Health Problems?

item.dc:creator: Michael Ungar Ph.D.

item.title: When Trauma Still Hurts: Memory Rescripting
item.description: When fear is rooted in memories of helplessness, working only in the present may not be enough. The memory rescripting technique can sometimes lead to fast and lasting recovery.

media:content.media:description: When Trauma Still Hurts: Memory Rescripting

item.dc:creator: David Burns M.D.

item.title: When Friends Fall Ill: The Psychological Angle
item.description: Personal Perspective: What happens to us when our friends fall ill? Do we respond empathically? Or do we unconsciously act out our fears and difficulties with infirmity?

media:content.media:description: When Friends Fall Ill: The Psychological Angle

item.dc:creator: Joan K. Peters Ph.D.

item.title: Building a Therapeutic Revolution: Veterans Lead the Way
item.description: As innovation accelerates, outcomes still lag. Veteran care highlights how coordinated systems may be key to advancing mental health treatment.

media:content.media:description: Building a Therapeutic Revolution: Veterans Lead the Way

item.dc:creator: Adi Zuloff-Shani Ph.D.

item.title: Hoarding Disorder May Lead to Increased Suicide Risk
item.description: Studies are revealing the link between suicide and hoarding disorders. Often those with hoarding disorders report depression, suicidal thoughts and behaviour, and social isolation.

media:content.media:description: Hoarding Disorder May Lead to Increased Suicide Risk

item.dc:creator: Robert T Muller Ph.D.

item.title: When Your Mind Turns Against You
item.description: Even your sharpest problem-solving skills can backfire. Learn how to turn relentless self-critique into clarity, confidence, and empowerment.

media:content.media:description: When Your Mind Turns Against You

item.dc:creator: Ryan C. Warner Ph.D.

item.title: How Childhood Trauma Impacts Our Sense of Trust
item.description: Why does trust feel so fragile for some people? Childhood trauma can shape how we relate to uncertainty, what we anticipate from others, and how we experience ourselves.

media:content.media:description: How Childhood Trauma Impacts Our Sense of Trust

item.dc:creator: Santiago Delboy MBA, LCSW

item.title: Using a Beginner's Mind
item.description: Being focused on what is occurring and blending within a given context provides a springboard for harmonious interaction with all relationships. Bending time is a process of converting its restrictions into a recognition of our ecological interdependence.

media:content.media:description: Mindfulness

item.dc:creator: Kenneth Silvestri Ed.D.

item.title: What Gossip and Small Talk Means for the Neurodiverse
item.description: People who are neurodivergent may struggle with gossip and small talk. This blog gives some reasons why this might be the case.

media:content.media:description: What Gossip and Small Talk Means for the Neurodiverse

item.dc:creator: Louise Taylor Ph.D.

item.title: New Findings About Some Women's Release of Fluid on Orgasm
item.description: Some women release a little or a lot of fluid on orgasm. Many release nothing. Either way, many women and their partners feel distressed. Is it normal?

media:content.media:description: New Findings About Some Women's Release of Fluid on Orgasm

item.dc:creator: Michael Castleman M.A.

item.title: He Never Hit Her. Then He Killed Her
item.description: A new study says domestic violence cases are less likely to end in homicide. Here's why that finding is more unsettling than reassuring.

media:content.media:description: He Never Hit Her. Then He Killed Her

item.dc:creator: Joni E Johnston Psy.D.

item.title: Has the Strength Model of Self-Control Been Depleted?
item.description: Reflecting on the strength model of self-control, some criticisms of the model, and some of the adjustments that have emerged.

media:content.media:description: Has the Strength Model of Self-Control Been Depleted?

item.dc:creator: Tamim Mobayed MA

item.title: Whose Mind Is It Anyway?
item.description: As artificial intelligence completes our thoughts, it can also begin to claim them.

media:content.media:description: Whose Mind Is It Anyway?

item.dc:creator: John Nosta

item.title: What the Anxious Generation Is Actually Missing
item.description: We focus on what screens are doing to young people. But the deeper question is what screens are replacing.

media:content.media:description: What the Anxious Generation Is Actually Missing

item.dc:creator: Pamela Cantor M.D.

item.title: The Manconomy Has a Body Image Problem Nobody Is Naming
item.description: From looksmaxxing forums to extreme bulk-and-cut cycles, male fitness culture has quietly developed a toxic relationship with appearance, and we keep calling it discipline.

media:content.media:description: The Manconomy Has a Body Image Problem Nobody Is Naming

item.dc:creator: Erin Parks Ph.D.

item.title: The Decision to Disclose a Hidden Disability at Work
item.description: Disabled employees reflect human diversity, not a reason for discrimination. Research explains.

media:content.media:description: The Decision to Disclose a Hidden Disability at Work
media:content.media:credit: Image by Institute of Professional Studies from Pixabay

item.dc:creator: Wendy L. Patrick, J.D., M.Div., Ph.D.

item.title: 3 Ways a Good Memory Becomes a Curse
item.description: Three ways the brain’s memory system sometimes trades detail for meaning.

media:content.media:description: 3 Ways a Good Memory Becomes a Curse

item.dc:creator: Mark Travers Ph.D.

item.title: My Schizophrenia Recovery Today
item.description: Personal Perspective: After my first hospital admission for schizophrenia, doctors determined I was permanently, totally disabled. They were wrong. It took a year, but I would fully recover.

media:content.media:description: Me today

item.dc:creator: Bethany Yeiser BS

item.title: Can Hyperbaric Oxygen Treat Psychiatric Disorders?
item.description: Could hyperbaric oxygen treat PTSD, depression, and anxiety? New evidence suggests that this treatment may rewire the brain—and the results are hard to ignore.

media:content.media:description: Can Hyperbaric Oxygen Treat Psychiatric Disorders?

item.dc:creator: Mitchell B. Liester M.D.

item.title: What if “What if" Thinking Is Good for Us?
item.description: "What if” thinking is a natural, adaptive cognitive process that supports learning and emotional resilience. We benefit more from accepting it than repressing it.

media:content.media:description: What if “What if" Thinking Is Good for Us?

item.dc:creator: Mike Verano LPC, LMFT

item.title: The Erasure That Altered Who "Counts" as Autistic
item.description: The "too articulate" people we’re now told "invaded" autism were there from the very start. The person who first described them in 1925 was erased from autism's history.

media:content.media:description: The Erasure That Altered Who "Counts" as Autistic

item.dc:creator: Ludmila N. Praslova, Ph.D.

item.title: Can Dogs Smell the Emotions of Other Dogs?
item.description: Dogs can recognize the emotional state of other dogs by scent alone, and their behavior changes in a manner consistent with their interpretation of that emotional odor.

media:content.media:description: Can Dogs Smell the Emotions of Other Dogs?

item.dc:creator: Stanley Coren PhD., DSc, FRSC

item.title: Aging as an Awakening
item.description: Aging creeps up on us. We are going about our lives, accomplishing and doing, and then all of a sudden, we find that we are receiving Medicare. Most are unprepared.

media:content.media:description: Aging as an Awakening

item.dc:creator: Diana Raab Ph.D.

item.title: Is Your Mind Getting in the Way of Your Memory?
item.description: People worry about losing their memory as they get older, but what most people don’t realize is that this worry can become a self-fulfilling prophecy unless you nip it in the bud.

media:content.media:description: Is Your Mind Getting in the Way of Your Memory?

item.dc:creator: Susan Krauss Whitbourne PhD, ABPP

item.title: The 6 Pillars of Brain Health
item.description: Boost your brain health with six science‑backed habits from sleep and exercise to nutrition and social connection that will enhance memory, mood, and cognitive vitality.

media:content.media:description: The 6 Pillars of Brain Health

item.dc:creator: Debra M. Kawahara Ph.D.

item.title: How Social, Cultural, and Political Structures Influence Our Feelings
item.description: How have modern values affected emotions?

media:content.media:description: How Social, Cultural, and Political Structures Influence Our Feelings

item.dc:creator: Glenn C. Altschuler Ph.D.

item.title: Why Our Brain Tells Us Horror Stories at Night
item.description: Sleep science now explains why the “mind after midnight” specialises in horror stories, and why we should never trust our night-time cognitions, which are driven by negativity bias

media:content.media:description: Why Our Brain Tells Us Horror Stories at Night

item.dc:creator: Anna Katharina Schaffner Ph.D.

item.title: Most Kids Don't Need Therapy: Here's What May Help More
item.description: Many parents think therapy is the solution when kids struggle. But the biggest lever for change might not be the child in therapy—it might be the parent.

media:content.media:description: Most Kids Don't Need Therapy: Here's What May Help More

item.dc:creator: Emily Edlynn Ph.D.
